coredata.dc:title: High-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) originated from China grass carp scales (CGCS) for effective detection of Hg(II) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Guanhong
Jia, Haishuang
Li, Na
Li, Xinyi
Yu, Zhiyue
Wang, Jun
Song, Youtao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) as a kind of environment-friendly fluorescent nanomaterial has been actively studied because of wide and potential applications, such as bioimaging and biosensors. It is worth mentioning that preparing CDs from the recycling of carbonaceous waste has received considerable research interest. We proposed a simple carbon dots synthesis technique, that is, using China grass carp scales (CGCS) as original and green materials by a one-step microwave hydrothermal method to prepare CDs. Since the mercury ion has strongly sulphophile, S atoms display higher thermodynamic affinity and faster integrating process with Hg2+ ions. Due to the presence of large amount of cysteine-containing sulfhydryl groups, the obtained CGCS-CDs can be used as a specific fluorescence probe for detecting Hg2+ ions. Subsequently, CGCS-CDs were characterized by HR-TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS analyses. The optical properties of CGCS-CDs were elucidated by fluorescence and UV–vis spectra. Additionally, the effects of pH values and salt concentrations on optical properties of CGCS-CDs were also researched by fluorescence spectra. Under optimal conditions, the decrease of fluorescence intensity displays a good linear relationship with allowable Hg2+ ion detection concentration range of 0.014–30 μmol/L and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.014 μmol/L is acquired. Meanwhile, the effect of other metal ions on the detection of Hg2+ ions by using CGCS-CDs as fluorescence probe was studied, suggesting that the CGCS-CDs could be an excellent fluorescence probe for the detection of Hg2+ ions. Eventually, the CGCS-CDs were proven to be low toxicity and applied for Hg2+ ions detection in lake water and cosmetic, demonstrating their potential towards diverse applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: China grass carp scales (CGCS)
Microwave hydrothermal
Carbon dots (CDs)
Sulfhydryl group
Nanoscale sensing
Mercury

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental details for cell imaging experiments
Preparation of lake water and cosmetic samples
TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS of CGCS-CDs
Fluorescence responses of CGCS-CDs to Hg2+ions
Principle and mechanism of detection Hg2+ by using CGCS-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Major Science and Technology Project of Water Pollution Control and Management in China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: China National Critical Project for Science and Technology on Water Pollution Prevention and Control

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly acknowledge the National Science Foundation of China ( 21371084 , 31570154 ), and Major Science and Technology Project of Water Pollution Control and Management in China ( 2015ZX07202-012 ) for financial support. The authors also thank our colleagues and other students for their participating in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Low-resolution TEM image, (b) High-resolution TEM image, (c) Size distribution histogram and (d) XRD spectra of CGCS-CDs.
FT-IR spectrum (a) and XPS patterns (b) of CGCS-CDs.
UV–vis absorption (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) (excited at 220 nm and 355 nm) of CGCS-CDs in aqueous solution.

Fluorescence emission spectra (a) (a-1: from 320 nm to 355 nm excitation wavelength at 10 nm intervals and a-2: from 355 nm to 400 nm excitation wavelength at 10 nm intervals) and fluorescence excitation spectra (b) of CGCS-CDs in aqueous solution.
Effects of solution acidity (pH) (a) and ionic strength (NaCl concentration) (b) on fluorescence intensities (at 355 nm) of CGCS-CDs solutions.

Comparison (a) of fluorescence intensities (at 355 nm) of CGCS-CDs + Hg2+ and CGCS-CDs solution at different pH values and effect of standing time (0–120 min) on fluorescence intensities (at 355 nm) of CGCS-CDs + Hg2+ solution.

Fluorescence spectra (a) of CGCS-CDs solutions at different Hg2+ concentrations (0–300 μmol/L) and difference values (b) of fluorescence intensities (ΔF) (at 355 nm) of CGCS-CDs + Hg2+ (F) and CGCS-CDs (F0) solutions at different Hg2+ concentrations (0–30 μmol/L).
Fluorescence spectra (a) (excited at 355 nm) and fluorescence intensities (b) (excited at 355 nm) of CGCS-CDs solutions in the presences of various metal ions ([Mn+] = 50 μmol/L).
The survival rate (a) and bright-field transmission image (b) of BV2 cells incubated with 100 mg/mL CGCS-CDs for 24 h at 37 °C.

Change of CGCS-CDs solution in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ion under visible light and ultraviolet light. (a: visible light, b: Hg2+ + visible light, c: ultraviolet light and d: Hg2+ + ultraviolet light.)
Detection mechanism of Hg2+ by using the CGCS-CDs as fluorescence probe.
Comparison of quantum yield of carbon dots prepared from different natural materials.
Comparison of experimental results using different fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.
Recoveries of Hg2+ ion in lake water and cosmetic (Toner) samples in the proposed method.

head.ce:title: High-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) originated from China grass carp scales (CGCS) for effective detection of Hg(II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Shengjing Hospital of China Medical University, Shenyang 110004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shengjing Hospital of China Medical University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: J. Wang, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a kind of environment-friendly fluorescent nanomaterial has been actively studied because of wide and potential applications, such as bioimaging and biosensors. It is worth mentioning that preparing CDs from the recycling of carbonaceous waste has received considerable research interest. We proposed a simple carbon dots synthesis technique, that is, using China grass carp scales (CGCS) as original and green materials by a one-step microwave hydrothermal method to prepare CDs. Since the mercury ion has strongly sulphophile, S atoms display higher thermodynamic affinity and faster integrating process with Hg2+ ions. Due to the presence of large amount of cysteine-containing sulfhydryl groups, the obtained CGCS-CDs can be used as a specific fluorescence probe for detecting Hg2+ ions. Subsequently, CGCS-CDs were characterized by HR-TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS analyses. The optical properties of CGCS-CDs were elucidated by fluorescence and UV–vis spectra. Additionally, the effects of pH values and salt concentrations on optical properties of CGCS-CDs were also researched by fluorescence spectra. Under optimal conditions, the decrease of fluorescence intensity displays a good linear relationship with allowable Hg2+ ion detection concentration range of 0.014–30 μmol/L and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.014 μmol/L is acquired. Meanwhile, the effect of other metal ions on the detection of Hg2+ ions by using CGCS-CDs as fluorescence probe was studied, suggesting that the CGCS-CDs could be an excellent fluorescence probe for the detection of Hg2+ ions. Eventually, the CGCS-CDs were proven to be low toxicity and applied for Hg2+ ions detection in lake water and cosmetic, demonstrating their potential towards diverse applications.

China grass carp scales (CGCS) are used as an original and green materials to prepare carbon dots (CDs) by a one-step microwave hydrothermal method. The obtained China grass carp scales-carbon dots (CGCS-CDs) can be used as a specific fluorescence probe in detection of Hg2+ ion for the first time. Under optimal conditions, the decrease of fluorescence intensity displays a good linear relationship in permitted detection concentration range of Hg2+ ion from 0.014 to 30 μmol/L and a limit of detection of 0.014 μmol/L was acquired. Meanwhile, the effect of other metal ions on the detection of Hg2+ ion by using CGCS-CDs as fluorescence probe was studied, suggesting that the CGCS-CDs could be an excellent fluorescence probe for the detection of Hg2+ ion. CGCS-CDs were proven to be low cytotoxicity and applied for Hg2+ ions detection in lake water and cosmetic, demonstrating their potential towards diverse applications.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg) is a kind of toxic elements which may carelessly enter the environment, as a result of the improper use and production. Mercury (Hg) has the characteristics of permanence, easy mobility, high biological enrichment and biological toxicity [1–3]. Particularly, the organomercury derivatives, as a special class of toxic and harmful pollutants, can not only exist for a long time in the air and food chain, also are able to make long distance migration [4,5]. At present, in some area near mercury (Hg) and non-ferrous metal smelting, the water, soil and rice may be detected to contain high concentration of Hg. Hg exists in nature mainly in three forms, that is, elemental mercury, inorganic mercury and organic mercury. Because of non-biodegradation, once the mercury compounds are released into the environment they are difficult to be destroyed [6,7]. For normal body the excess absorption of Hg2+ ion, through bio-concentration into the food chain, can damage the central nervous system, liver, kidney and intestinal and even lead to death [8]. How to control mercury pollution has become a hot spot, giving rise to the focus of global attention. The environmental risk management of mercury pollution in the global scope has been an irresistible trend. Thereby, it has become very important to track and detect Hg. In recent years, the methods of detecting Hg2+ ions prevailingly included atomic absorption spectrometry, spectrophotometry, high performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical method and fluorescence analysis [9–11]. As yet the fluorescence analysis method has attracted attention due to the merits of convenient, specific, sensitive and timely detection in situ.

Recently, the CDs have been actively studied because of wide and potential applications, including electrochemical catalysis, electrochemical sensing, energy conversion, bioimaging and biosensors [12–15]. It is worth mentioning that the CDs are a kind of environment-friendly fluorescent nanomaterials [16]. In addition, being similar to the conventional quantum dots, the CDs have good optical performance, small size and some other advantages [17–19]. These attractive properties, including low cytotoxicity biocompatibility, easy preparation and mild reaction conditions are different from those semiconductor-based quantum dots, such as CdS, CdTe and so on [20,21]. Acting as electron donors or acceptors, the CDs have a more strong ability to couple with their conjugates, accounting for their growing interest as candidates in a number of applications ranging from sensing and bioimaging.

Preparation approaches of CDs are generally classified into two categories, “top-down” and “bottom-up” [22]. The former has currently been widely used to exfoliate CDs at room temperature from larger carbon structural materials, such as graphite, carbon nanotubes, carbon soot, activated carbon and so on by using some methods like discharge, laser ablation and electrochemical oxidation [23–25]. On the other hand, the “bottom-up” approaches to prepare CDs is from macromolecular organic compounds, for instance, citrate, carbohydrates and polymer-silica nanocomposites as raw materials through combustion/thermal treatments, supported synthetic and microwave synthetic routes to obtain CDs [26–29]. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs achieved by the initial preparation is generally weak or the fluorescence quantum yield is low. These intrinsic disadvantages greatly limit their large-scale production and cannot meet the conditions for such diverse applications as fluorescent labeling or biological imaging. Many efforts have been devoted to enhance its capabilities in order to improve CDs based probe performance. Several bright methods, which are doping some elements into CDs and surface modification or passivation [30–32], have been extensively focused on owing to the remarkable improvements. For instance, enhancing sensitivity by using nitrogen doped CDs or nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs in the detection of Hg2+ ions have been reported in previous literatures [33]. As these studies provided a solid foundation for researchers to further explore, the potential of highly selective and sensitive CDs to detect Hg2+ ion has been tapped gradually. To a certain extent, this way can increase the quantum yield of CDs and enhance the specific recognition ability of detection. However, in turn, excessively deliberating doping could influence the CDs itself characteristic. Altering the composition of CDs maybe influence light absorbing ability and transforming the electronic structure of CDs also reduce the ability of self-luminous. It should be noticed that the compositions and fluorescence behaviors of the CDs derived from various methods all greatly depend on their precursors. Therefore, for the preparation of CDs with high quality without doping, the choice of carbon source is priority among priorities. The optimal precursors to prepare CDs might be those hydrosoluble aromatic compounds with rich amino and carboxyl groups [34]. Benzene ring structure by means of π-π stacking self-assembly principle is beneficial to the formation of carbon core structure, while amino and carboxyl groups will contribute to the formation of surface state. The surface state can proceed to encourage the fluorescence center promoted by a synergetic hybridization structure of carbon core and its surface chemical groups. So, the raw material pursuing of novel biomass for CDs is still intriguing and attractive.

In this work, a new carbon source, China grass carp scales (CGCS), was chosen for the preparation of CDs without using any passivation reagents. In CGCS there are plenty of protein and ash, as well as a small amount of fat and carbohydrate. Especially, there are a large number of cysteines with sulfhydryl group. Because the Hg2+ ion has strongly sulphophile, the CGCS as carbon source, using an easy one-step microwave hydrothermal synthesis method, were used to prepare the China grass carp scales‑carbon dots (CGCS-CDs) to detect Hg2+ ion. This preparation method avoids the possible troubles from co-doping, such as complicated experimental step and worry that the doped atoms cannot enter CDs smoothly. The prepared CGCS-CDs were characterized by High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-Ray Powder Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses. The optical properties of CGCS-CDs were elucidated by fluorescence and UV–vis spectra. Additionally, the effects of pH values and salt concentrations on optical properties of CGCS-CDs were also researched by means of fluorescence spectra. The processes and possible mechanism of Hg2+ detection are proposed. It is expected that the prepared CGCS-CDs as fluorescence probe would be a sensitive and selective tool for the rapid Hg2+ detection in bioanalysis.

The microwave hydrothermal synthesis instrument (XH-800G, Xianghu Science and Technology Ltd., China) was used to prepare CGCS-CDs. The size and morphology of the CGCS-CDs were examined by using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECNAI F20 field-emission, Microscopy and Analytical Imaging Laboratory, USA) operated at 200 kV. The tested sample was prepared by dropping water dispersion of sample onto carbon-coated copper TEM grids using pipettes and dried under ambient condition. X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained from a X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, D-8, Bruker-axs, Germany, Ni filtered Cu Kα radiation in the range of 2θ from 10° to 70°). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy spectra were measured by a FT-IR spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet 5700, Nicolet Company, USA) with the KBr pellet technique ranging from 4000 to 500 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements were performed on a photoelectron instrument (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250, Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) equipped with Al Kα radiation. The binding energy was calibrated against the carbon 1 s line. All fluorescence measurements were performed on fluorophotometer (Cary 300, Varian Company, USA) with excitation slit set at 10 nm band pass and emission at 5 nm band pass in 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm quartz cell. The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cary 50, Varian Company, USA). The light of 365 nm from an ultraviolet lamp was used to irradiate CGCS-CDs solution. A Global digital pH meter (FE20K, Mettler-Toledo Company, Switzerland) was used to measure the pH values of the aqueous solutions.

The China grass carp scales (CGCS) were bought from seafood supermarket (Shenyang, China) to prepare the CDs. Quinine sulfate (analytically pure, Shanghai Chemical Reagent Factory, China) as a reference to calculate the fluorescence quantum yield of CGCS-CDs. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, analytically pure), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, analytically pure) and sodium chloride (NaCl, analytically pure) (Shenyang Chemical Reagent Plant, China) were used to adjust the pH and salt concentration of solution. Standard Hg2+ ion stock solution (1000 μmol/L) was prepared by dissolving Hg(NO3)2 (analytically pure, Jiangyan Universal Reagent Factory, China) in ultrapure water. Other metal ion solutions (100 μmol/L) as the interference ions obtained by dissolving the corresponding metal salts (analytically pure, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China) were prepared in ultrapure water. All the reagents and chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. And all aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water supplied by a Water Purifier Nano pure water system (18.3 MΩ cm, Hitech-sciencetool, Shanghai, China). Lake water samples were obtained from the Lake of the Beiling Park (Shenyang, China).

According to the process as shown in Scheme 1 , the preparation of CDs by using China grass carp scales (CGCS) as raw material was carried out. Washing the CGCS repeatedly, tiling in the stainless steel plate under ventilated place until them dry naturally then cut them into small pieces. Mixing these grass scale pieces with ultrapure water, the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. By using one-step microwave hydrothermal synthesis with constant heat temperature at 200 °C for 2.00 h, cooling to room temperature. A Buchner funnel filter was used to remove the unreacted grass carp scale pieces. After filtrated, the synthesized CGCS-CDs solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant yellow-brown transparent solution after centrifugation was collected and dialyzed against ultra-pure water through a dialysis membrane (3500 Da) for 24 h, changing the ultra-pure water every 8.0 h. Then, the dialyzed CGCS-CD solution in dialysis bag was collected into a volumetric flask and stored in a refrigerator at 4.0 °C.

The quantum yield (QY) of the CGCS-CDs is calculated according to an established procedure. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.10 mol/L H2SO4 solution is chosen as a standard reference sample. The fixed and known fluorescence quantum yield value is about 0.54, when the excitation wavelength is 360 nm. To minimize the re-absorption effects, absorbency in the fluorescence cuvette was kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength of 355 nm. The quantum yield of the CGCS-CDs is determined at the excitation wavelength of 355 nm by the following equation:
(1)
Y =
Y Q
I
I Q
A Q
A
N
N Q
2
where Y represents the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the optical density and N is the refractive index of the solution. Quinine sulfate is labeled as the subscript Q. So, through this equation, we can successfully know the quantum yield of CGCS-CDs is 19.92%. At the same time, CGCS-CDs are compared to other CDs whose carbon sources are also from the natural material (Table 1 ). The quantum yield of CGCS-CDs is relatively higher than that of others, indicating that, the CGCS can be adopted as a new type of carbon source and can also acquire a higher quantum yield.

The detection of Hg2+ ions was performed in ultrapure water solution. Firstly, an appropriate volume of Hg2+ ions as working solution were diluted to the required concentration (0–500 μmol/L) with ultrapure water. Then, 2.00 mL CGCS-CDs aqueous solution having 2.00 mL different concentrations of Hg2+ ions solution was thoroughly mixed. Finally, the mixed solutions were added in a quartz cuvette in turn, and then the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm were recorded subsequently. The peculiar selectivity of the CGCS-CDs for the detection of Hg2+ ions was confirmed by adding other metal ion stock solutions instead of Hg2+ ions in a similar way. All experiments were performed at the room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental details for cell imaging experiments
ce:section.ce:para: BV2 cells were cultured in high sugar medium of (5% fetal bovine serum and 1% Penicillin-Streptomycin solution) in an incubator (37 °C, 5% CO 2/95% air). For imaging, BV2 cells were seeded on a coverslip in 6-well plate 12 h before use. The culture medium was replaced by 2.5 mL fresh medium containing 100 mg/mL CGCS-CDs and the cells were incubated for another 24 h. After being washed with fresh PBS (pH = 7.0) three times, the cells were imaged on the inverted microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lake water and cosmetic samples
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, the lake water and cosmetic samples were filtered and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min before evaluating. The resultant water samples were spiked with Hg2+ ion at different concentration levels, incubated at room temperature for 10 min and then analyzed with the proposed method. Next, the prepared samples were used for detection of Hg2+ ions according to the general procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS of CGCS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 1(a), a full view of TEM image of the bare CGCS-CDs can be found. The CGCS-CDs with spherical shape are well-separated without any apparent aggregation. The image contrast is not quite obvious, showing that they may be amorphous carbons. And the high-resolution TEM image Fig. 1(b) reveals a highly micro-crystalline feature of CGCS-CDs, with a crystalline plane spacing of 0.34 nm. One hundred particles were randomly selected to measure the particle size and the size distribution histogram was obtained. The corresponding distribution histogram of the particle size is shown in Fig. 1(c). It can be found that the average diameters mainly distribute in the range of 2.6 ± 0.8 nm and the diameter of a quarter of CGCS-CDs is about 1.8 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of Fig. 1(d) reveals a diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 23° for CGCS-CDs, indicating that the CGCS-CDs are highly disordered carbon atoms. It can be confirmed that the CGCS-CDs are similar to those reported CDs with graphene-like structure [35]. The powder XRD pattern of CGCS-CDs reveals a broad diffraction band at approximately 23°, corresponding to the (002) crystal planes with 0.34 nm spacing. It demonstrates again that the CGCS-CDs have weakly graphitic crystallinity. This kind of behavior has been observed in previously literature [36], that is, the CGCS-CDs are highly amorphous essentially but do possess a slight graphitic nature.

The studies on Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy are carried out to obtain chemical and structural information of the CGCS-CDs. As revealed in Fig. 2(a), the CGCS-CDs exhibit a broad peak at 3412 cm−1, where is evidenced by the absorption bands of OH and NH stretching vibrations. And the absorption peak at 1652 cm−1 is attributed to carboxylic acid and CC stretching vibrations. The stretching vibration of COC bonds appears at 1047 cm−1. The above analyses of FT-IR can affirm the presence of abundant oxygen containing functional groups and also demonstrate that the CGCS-CDs have excellent water-solubility [37]. Methyl and methylene absorption peaks from the protein and amino compounds in original CGCS are observable at 2354.2 cm−1. The peaks at 2968 cm−1 and 1451 cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristic bending vibration of saturated hydrocarbon CH and CH2 , respectively. It collectively shows the presence of the alkyl and alkene groups. The obtained basic compositions and structures tally with the previously reported CDs. Simultaneously, the peaks at 1549 nm−1 are related to –NH2, which generally is found in N-doped carbon dots [38]. It is noted that the vibration bands of the nitrogen-containing functional groups can also be seen in this FT-IR spectrum. Particularly, a relatively strong peak appears at 675 cm−1, which is related to the characteristic peak of CS bond, indicating that the sulfhydryl groups are contained in CGCS. It has excellently been reserved in the design and preparation of CGCS-CDs. That is also what we want to get. It provides a great advantage for the next step to specifically detect Hg2+ ion.

Detailed elemental analyses of CGCS-CDs have also been unveiled by XPS measurements. The results show that the CGCS-CDs give three peaks at 285.08 eV, 399.73 eV and 532.07 eV (Fig. 2(b)), which correspond to the C(1S), N(1S) and O(1S), respectively. The C(1S) spectrum (Fig. 2(b-1)) can be further divided into five peaks at 283.3 eV (sp2 C), 284.8 eV (sp3 C), 285.3 eV (CS), 286.4 eV (CO) and 287.4 eV (CN/CO), respectively [39]. The N(1S) spectrum (Fig. 2(b-2)) shows the presence of pyridine N (399.3 eV) and graphitic N (401.2 eV) [40]. Especially, for this CGCS-CDs, two new peaks are observed at 228.0 eV and 168.0 eV, corresponding to S(2 s) (Fig. 2(b-3)) and S(2p) (Fig. 2(b-4)), respectively. The S atoms contained in CGCS-CDs take the form of CSC, as evidenced by the spectrum of S(2p), the peak centered at 168.0 eV, corresponding to S(2P1/2) of thiophene S [41]. The XPS analyses clearly demonstrate that the S atoms derive from the cysteine of CGCS, which enters into the framework of CGCS-CDs through the microwave hydrothermal treatment, and some functional groups in CGCS-CDs like carboxyl and hydroxyl groups are also well reserved.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CGCS-CDs in aqueous solution were determined to explore their optical properties. In the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3(a)), the CGCS-CDs in aqueous solution show one absorption peak and one shoulder peak in ultraviolet range at 220 nm and 287 nm, respectively. They should be attributable to π - π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and the n - π* transition of CO bond, respectively. The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3(b)) were obtained under the excitation of 220 nm and 355 nm wavelength lights. The results showed that the fluorescence spectrum excited at 220 nm gives a strong and pointy emission peak around 545 nm. The emission peak excited at 220 nm looks pointy, implying that the resonance fluorescence peak is less likely to be disturbed. In addition, the portable ultraviolet (UV) lamp wavelength is usually immobilized around 360 nm. Therefore, the excitation wavelength of 355 nm has practical application value. For comparison the fluorescence spectrum excited at 355 nm was also given in Fig. 3(b). The fluorescence intensity excited at 220 nm is slightly higher than that excited at 355 nm. And that, the emission peak excited at 220 nm appears at 545 nm towards more visible light, which can be observed by the naked eye. So, the excitation wavelength of 220 nm is also meaningful. That is, the Hg2+ ion in actual sample can not only be detected fast by using 360 nm wavelength ultraviolet (UV) lamp in the outdoor, but also the sample containing Hg2+ ion can be taken back to the laboratory for a meticulous examination by using corresponding instrument with 220 nm wavelength.

The optical properties of prepared CGCS-CDs were further investigated by fluorescence spectra, and the corresponding maximum excitation and emission spectra were initially recorded as 355 nm and 441 nm, respectively. To address whether the prepared CGCS-CDs showed the dependence of emission peaks on excitation wavelength, the corresponding normalized fluorescence spectra were depicted in Fig. 4(a). The gradual red shift and increasing fluorescence intensity patterns in the emission spectra were found with increasing excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 355 nm (Fig. 4(a-1)). The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at 355 nm excitation wavelength. After that, the fluorescence intensity began to decline with the varying of excitation wavelengths from 355 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 4(a-2)). The results were consistent with previous observations, where the CDs also exhibited an emission dependence on excitation pattern [42]. Besides, the obtained CGCS-CDs excited by 800 nm wavelength light showed the maximum up-converted emission peak at 415 nm, suggesting their up-conversion property, likely due to its anti-Stokes photoluminescence [43]. At the same time, we changed the emission wavelength value to find maximum excitation peak (Fig. 4(b)). It was confirmed that the 355 nm was the optimum excitation wavelength. Thus, as the following research of fluorescence spectra in this study the 355 nm was used as the optimum excitation wavelength. Through fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra, it can be testified that the optical characteristics of CGCS-CDs are in accord with those of common CDs [44], owning a wide and continuous excitation spectrum and a narrow and symmetry emission spectrum.

Furthermore, the influences of solution pH and ionic strength on fluorescent spectra of CGCS-CDs in aqueous solution were also studied. The fluorescence response of CGCS-CDs at different pH could be observed in Fig. 5(a). It can be clearly seen that the CGCS-CDs display low fluorescence intensities at low and high pH value ranges. That is, under strong acid and strong alkali conditions, the CGCS-CDs hardly give fluorescence emissions.

The fluorescence intensity increased gradually along with the increasing of pH value, reaching the highest position at pH = 6.00. Continuing to raise the pH value, the fluorescence intensity tended to decline. Therefore, the prepared CGCS-CDs can show relatively high fluorescence intensities at pH = 6.00–9.00, which can be acted as one of the conditions for Hg2+ ion detection. Apparently, there are many carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of prepared CGCS-CDs, which display a high sensitivity to solution pH.

The physiological or clinical sodium chloride (NaCl) concentrations are usually fixed in 0.85–0.90% for mammals and human body and 0.67–0.70% for amphibious class animals, respectively, which is necessary to maintain the normal osmotic pressure. So, as another influence factor on fluorescent spectra of CGCS-CDs in aqueous solution was also investigated. In Fig. 5(b), the results showed that the fluorescence intensity excited at 355 nm wavelength nearly unchanged at high ionic strength up to 1.00 mol/L. Apparently, the emissive property of CGCS-CDs is insensitive to the change of ionic strength, suggesting that the prepared CGCS-CDs could be applied to biological imaging. Besides, the prepared CGCS-CDs stored for more than four months still manifested almost identical emission spectra as the freshly prepared samples, indicating the CGCS-CDs are highly stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of CGCS-CDs to Hg2+ions
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the detection conditions, some comparative experiments were carried out. Solution pH is one of the key factors in the detection system. Acid and alkali may also change the surface state of CGCS-CDs, of course, there will bring some knock-on effects on the detecting efficiency and accuracy from conditional disparity. The detection efficiencies in neutral pH solutions are superior to that in strong acid and strong alkaline conditions. The details in pH range of 6.0–9.0 are described in Fig. 6(a), the tendency of detection rate increased after slightly decreased following the status of pH sustained went up. Taking into account the CGCS-CDs pH is 6 actually, in order to maintain its own natural condition, pH = 6.00 is more conducive to get a better detection effect without changing pH. Although the fluorescence quenching rate of the probe is different in various pH conditions, it affect slightly in acidic, alkaline and neutral environment for the identification of Hg2+, which displays grass carp scales as raw materials for the preparation of CGCS-CDs solution are able to detect Hg2+ ions in a broader region.

We have also studied the effect of response time on the fluorescence quenched efficiency (Fig. 6(b)). The results reveal that the fluorescence quenching could reach a stable value within 10 min and that the system is still stable in 2.0 h, indicating that the binding of CGCS-CDs and Hg2+ ion is considerably stable. These findings are in favor of the application in the actual environment. With the purpose of obtaining a lower detection limit, pH = 6.00 and 10 min response time are chosen as experimental conditions in the following studies.

For the detection of Hg2+ ion, different amounts of mercury nitrate solutions were added into CGCS-CDs solution. The solutions were incubated for 10 min, and then the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm. As shown in the Fig. 7(a), the corresponding fluorescence spectra indicate that the addition of Hg2+ gradually decreases the fluorescence intensity of the CGCS-CDs solution at 355 nm, but has no effect on the position of fluorescence peaks. While the Hg2+ ion concentration reaches 300 μmol/L, the intensity begins to unchanged. It implies that the combination of CGCS-CDs and Hg2+ ions have reached saturation point. Moreover, Fig. 7(b) gives the plots of the quenched fluorescence intensities (ΔF) (ΔF = F0 − F, where F0 and F respectively represent the fluorescence intensity of CGCS-CDs and CGCS-CDs + Hg2+) against the concentration of Hg2+ ion. With the increase of Hg2+ ion concentration, the ΔF increases gradually, showing a good linear relationship within the range of 0–30 μmol/L. The obtained linear regression equation is ∆F = 1.9103C + 0.5024 and the correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.9951 at 3 signal-to-noise ratio (S/N), where C is the concentration of Hg2+ ion. The limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ ion using the developed fluorescence probe (CGCS-CDs) was determined to be 0.014 μmol/L. The determination for given solutions which containing 0.50 μmol/L Hg2+ ions were repeated 5 times, and the obtained relative standard deviation is 8.94%. It indicates that the method has good reproducibility. In addition, the experimental results obtained by this method were compared with those reported previously (Table 2 ).

Hg2+ ion can generally coexist with various cations in natural water. Moreover, some elements in the same period or in the same main group in periodic table of the elements with Hg2+ ion have similar attributes and share a commonality. Many of them can also quench the fluorescence of ordinary CDs via nonradioactive electron-transfer. For this reason, it is critical to investigate the selectivity and identifiability of the CGCS-CDs. Whether other metal ions have effects on the fluorescence intensity of CGCS-CDs, especially, metal cations whose properties are similar to Hg2+ ion are explored through the fluorescence intensity comparison (Fig. 8 ). After the addition of Hg2+ and other metal ions, the fluorescence emission peaks of CGCS-CDs recorded at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm had no displacement, only their fluorescence intensities were different from one another. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the added Hg2+ ion dramatically decreased the fluorescence intensity of CGCS-CDs to a half, and the rest of metal ions had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of CGCS-CDs, which could be almost neglected. Particularly, from Fig. 8(b) it can be found that the as-proposed probe shows high selectivity towards Hg2+ ion over other metallic ions due to the advantage of the sulphophile-specificity, which causes a special affinity with Hg2+ ion. Additionally, such sensing system features also demonstrate the simplicity, rapidity and flexibility in the detection of Hg2+ ion.

To evaluate cytotoxicity of CGCS-CDs, a MTT assay on BV2 cells and CGCS-CDs mixed solution as an example was performed and the results were given in Fig. 9 . After BV2 cells in the presence of CGCS-CDs were incubated in culture medium for 24 h and washed by using fresh PBS (pH = 7.0) three times, it is observed from the obtained result that the cell survivability hardly decreases even up to 100 mg/mL of CGCS-CDs. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the estimated cellular viability was about 96%, exhibiting low toxicity to BV2 cells. As can be seen in Fig. 9(b), even though there is a great change in such medium, the BV2 cells maintain normal shape and have no deformity. In view of the above-mentioned low toxicity, it can be predicted that the CGCS-CDs arouses growing interest as fluorescent probe in bioimaging application for monitoring of Hg2+ ions in living cells.

For checking the practicality of CGCS-CDs in factual detection of Hg2+ ion, the standard recovery experiments were performed for lake water and cosmetic samples. After adding different concentrations of Hg2+ ions (1.5, 2.5 and 3.0 μmol/L) into three lake water samples, respectively, the detected amounts of Hg2+ ions are 1.600, 2.590 and 2.921 μmol/L. After adding different concentrations of Hg2+ ions (1.0, 2.0 and 3.0 μmol/L) into three cosmetic samples, the detected amounts of Hg2+ ions are 0.966, 1.912 and 2.632 μmol/L, respectively. For used lake water and cosmetic samples, the background values of Hg2+ ions were tested as 1.01 × 10−5 μmol/L and 3.3 × 10−5 μmol/L, respectively. Aaccording to the formula, R (Recovery) = (C2 − C1) / C3 × 100% (where C1, C2 and C3 represent the background value, determined value and spiked value of Hg2+ ions, respectively), the corresponding recoveries can be calculated. The satisfactory recoveries between 90.72% and 106.67% were obtained for lake water and cosmetic samples (see Table 3 ). The results suggest that this method can be straight forwardly implemented for the assay of Hg2+ ion detection in real samples, showing a good accuracy and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and mechanism of detection Hg2+ by using CGCS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 shows the photographs of CGCS-CDs in the presence and absence of Hg2+ ion under sun light and ultraviolet light (365 nm), respectively. It is clear that there are significant differences in color for these samples. In the absence of Hg2+ ion, the CGCS-CDs exhibit maple (Fig. 10(a)) and bright green (Fig. 10(c)) under sunlight and ultraviolet light (365 nm), respectively. It displays that the CGCS-CDs can emit strong green fluorescence under the exposure of ultraviolet light (365 nm). After adding the Hg2+ ion, the color of CGCS-CDs changes from maple to light yellow under sun light (Fig. 10(b)). However, the color of CGCS-CDs solution changes from green to lightless under ultraviolet light (365 nm) (Fig. 10(d)), indicating that the Hg2+-induced fluorescence quenching has happened, which can be directly observed in naked-eye.

It is well known that the fluorescence emissions of most CDs presumably arise from the radiative recombination of excitons trapped by the surface defects, which can be quenched by metal ions such as Hg2+, Pb2+ or Cu2+ via nonradiative electron-transfer between CDs and metal ions [45]. For the CGCS-CDs, they also contain abundant functional groups such as oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CGCS-CDs, which can introduce a plenty of defects to serve as the excitation energy traps, and also coordinate with Hg2+ to form complexes, leading to the electrons transfer from CGCS-CDs to Hg2+ (Fig. 11 ).

Generally, the combination of dynamic model will occur as soon as the common CDs encounter the Hg2+ ions. The combination of this form can only give a slight quenching of fluorescence intensity caused by a continuous collision between each other. Therefore, it fails to be a tight combination. But for CGCS-CDs, the fluorescence intensity is apparently reduced after adding the Hg2+ ions.

According to the previous reports [46], the doping of S atoms can facilitate the electron transfer. S atoms as electron donor have smaller electronegativity (2.58) and larger atomic radius, leading to the valence electrons of S atoms in the third shell losing easily. On the one hand, the preservation of S atoms in the CGCS-CDs should effectively tune the electronic local density of CGCS-CDs, which will promote the coordination interaction between oxygen functional groups on the edge of CGCS-CDs and Hg2+ ions. On the other hand, the Hg2+ ions have the strongly sulphophile and the distinctive selectivity, due to the higher thermodynamic affinity and faster integrating process with S atoms on the surface of CGCS-CDs. It is prone to combine with the CS included in CGCS-CDs, forming the S-Hg2+-S hairpin structures [28,47], which contributes to bring the specific detection of Hg2+ by using CGCS-CDs (Fig. 11). Taking advantage of high natural sulfur content contained in the CGCS, the preparation of CGCS-CDs not only introduce S atoms deriving from cysteine in CGCS, but also produce no adverse by-products. Consequently, the CGCS-CDs show good detection sensitivity and selectivity as fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection.

In summary, this is the first time to utilize CGCS which have a large number of cysteines containing sulfhydryl group as precursor for preparing the CDs to detect Hg2+ ion. The CGCS-CDs obtained from renewable bio-precursors CGCS via microwave hydrothermal method exhibit various advantages including high fluorescent quantum yield (19.92%), pH-sensitivity and satisfactory stability at a high salt concentration. The fluorescent probe also shows low cytotoxicity and good cell-permeability. The prepared CGCS-CDs can emit bright green light under ultraviolet light irradiation. Because the Hg2+ ion has strongly sulphophile, the S atoms which derived from the cysteines of CGCS can effectively combine with the Hg2+ ion by forming the S–Hg2+–S hairpin structures. The prepared CGCS-CDs can successfully be exploited as potential platforms for assaying Hg2+ ion based on the combination with COOH, OH, NH2 and CS of CGCS-CDs directly, which facilitates to quench their fluorescence. This strategy provides a promising probe for specific detection of Hg2+ ion without any further chemical modification. Additionally, the best detection result can be achieved at pH = 6.00. Under the optimal condition, the decrease of fluorescence intensity is linear with the increase of Hg2+ ion concentration in the range of 0.014–30 μmol/L. In this concentration range the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.014 μmol/L was obtained. More importantly, other metal ions have scarcely any interference in the detection of Hg2+ ion by using CGCS-CDs, suggesting that the CGCS-CDs can combine with Hg2+ ion specifically and availably. This study fully elaborates that the China grass carp scales are useable natural raw materials for CDs preparation, offering a significantly valuable probe for Hg2+ ion detection. It is expected that this strategy may offer a promising and green, low toxicity and sensitive probe to detect Hg2+ ion in environmental, imaging, bio-sensing applications and so on.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the National Science Foundation of China (21371084, 31570154), and Major Science and Technology Project of Water Pollution Control and Management in China (2015ZX07202-012) for financial support. The authors also thank our colleagues and other students for their participating in this work.
